content:
# Top 5 posts from r/MovieSuggestions day

## 1. A good movie to watch with a 11 year old girl and 15 year old boy?

**Score:** 67
**Body:** I've got 2 kids staying with me this evening and want to watch a movie that they'l both love. They reckon this is impossible but I'm certain it is possible! But would love to hear any suggestions please. 

### Top 10 comments and their replies (up to depth 10)

1. Second hand lions
2. Cool runnings

3. Goonies.
4. School of Rock
5. Holes, absolutely. On Disney
6. Princess Bride, perhaps. Depends on the type of 15 year old boy 
  - Is this a kissing movie*?
7. Jurassic Park
8. Zathura
9. Princess Bride, Young Frankenstein 

## 2. Please suggest a good prison escape movie

**Score:** 37
**Body:** I have watched a lot of them, specially the popular ones in English language.

Could you suggest at least one, which has an IMDB rating of 5.0 at least?

**Edit:**

So i have watched these -

* The Shawshank Redemption
* Escape from Alcatraz
* Papillon (Both)
* A Man Escaped
* Midnight Express
* Escape from Sobibor
* The McKenzie break (1970)
* Stalag 17
* O Brother, Where Art Thou?
* The Count of Monte Cristo (Both)
* Rescue Dawn
* The Way Back
* The Green Mile
* Breakout
* The Last Escape
* The Colditz Story
* Escape from Pretoria
* Escape Plan
* Escape Plan 2: Hades
* Escape Plan: The Extractors
* Brute Force
* Le Trou
* Cool Hand Luke
* No Escape
* Victory
* Chicken Run
* Escape Room

Maybe a few more i can't remember.

### Top 10 comments and their replies (up to depth 10)

1. The great escape
  - Thinking outside of the box. Excellent!
2. Escape from Alcatraz. It's awesome!
3. Tango and Cash if you're looking for some nice 80s cheese

Lock Up if you wanna have a Stallone marathon pair that with Tango and Cash

The Last Castle with Robert Redford and James Gandolfini


4. Escape from New York
5. Papillion.. original there's also a remake 
6. I'm sure you've already watched Shawshank Redemption. But if you haven't, you should.
7. Papilon 
8. Midnight Express! 
9. Down By Law

## 3. Interactive movie options

**Score:** 21
**Body:** Hi there!

I'm a high school teacher and last year I did an "interactive showing" of Twilight in honor of the 20th anniversary of the book.

Basically there were actions that the audience did when certain things happened (howling every time Jacob appeared on screen, opening your eyes really wide whenever someone was staring, etc.)

It was AMAZING. It was in the evening so we opened it up to the community. Teens came out but so did some 20-somethings and even several moms.

I want to do another interactive movie this year because it was so great.

But... I'm having trouble figuring out what to show.

I really want it to be intergenerational-teen like Twilight was. This will be taking place in October so Halloween movies are an option but not required.

Options I have now are:

\- Legally Blonde

\- Hocus Pocus

\- Mamma Mia

Help a teacher out! Give your ideas!

(NOT Princess Bride though... The 40th anniversary is next year so I'll be showing it then)

TIA!

### Top 10 comments and their replies (up to depth 10)

1. The Goonies

Clueless
2. Clue would be fun.
3. Hocus Pocus!!!! 
4. Mamma Mia. The second the songs start, everyone joins in anyway.
5. Isn’t this what they do for showings of Rocky Horror Picture Show? 
  - Similar!  Not really appropriate for a school showing, though.
6. Maybe High School Musical?
7. What a cute idea! You should do one for Christmas too!

Any of the Star Wars.

Charlie and the Chocolate Factory

Wizard of Oz

Scream or any number of campy horror movies

Jurassic Park

Really the possibilities are endless. It depends on whether you are going for campy movies or ones that are also enjoyable for people.
8. hocus pocus is probably your safest bet since it already has that built-in energy where people want to yell stuff at the screen and it fits the october timing perfectly, but honestly mamma mia might actually be the move here because once those songs kick in your audience will basically do the interactive part for you without needing a ruleset. everyone just starts singing and dancing in their seats whether you tell them to or not. i went to a community screening of it a few years back and it was one of the most fun theater experiences i've had, watching a 70-year-old woman next to me lose it during dancing queen. the intergenerational appeal is already there since like every age group has that one mamma mia song that gets them. legally blonde could work too but mamma mia just has that automatic participation vibe that makes the interactive element feel effortless for people who might otherwise feel self-conscious about yelling at a screen.
9. Mean Girls 

## 4. What’s an underrated movie you think deserves way more attention?

**Score:** 18
**Body:** Not talking about obscure indie films nobody has heard of, I mean movies that did come out, maybe had decent attention, but somehow just… faded away or never really got their due. Sometimes I feel like a movie will get released, people will talk about it for a week, and then it disappears from everyone’s memory like it never existed. Meanwhile, it could be one of the more interesting or well-made films in its genre.

I’ve also noticed that some movies kind of get quietly re-evaluated years later, but others stay stuck in this “meh / forgettable” category forever, even if they’re way better than that label suggests. Anyway, I’m curious what everyone else thinks. What’s a movie you believe deserves a better reputation than it has right now?

### Top 10 comments and their replies (up to depth 10)

1. Searching for Bobby Fischer 
2. The Wailing (2016, Korean) technically horror but it operates more like a mystery that keeps pulling the rug out. Every time you think you understand what's happening the film shifts again. One of the best genre films of the last decade that never broke through to mainstream recognition outside of enthusiast circles.
3. Breakdown 
4. American Honey

Girl in abusive home runs away after meeting traveling group of teenagers selling magazines door to door.

It’s a slice of life of her first real taste of freedom and heading into a completely unknown future.

Also featured mostly amateur actors pulled off the streets and just feels very authentic.

My fav movie to recommend to people because so few have seen it
5. Equilibrium (Christian Bale)
6. Dungeons & Dragons: Honor Amongst Thieves

One of the most genuiely laugh-out-loud blockbusters in recent years. Suffered with poor promotion and being up against the juggernaut of the Super Mario Movie

And it’s *so* fun
7. The Red Violin (1998). It’s a movie about the history of a violin. Sounds boring, but it’s very good and stars Samuel L. Jackson. 
8. Moon (2009) with Sam Rockwell. One of the most interesting sci-fi movies I watched without any expectations and was blown away.
9. Sahara (2005). It's a super fun action adventure. I have never seen anyone talking about it online even though it has 100k imdb ratings.

## 5. I’m Not Saying I Want a Boring Movie, but….

**Score:** 11
**Body:** I genuinely don’t want the cinematic version of someone reading me the owner’s manual from a 1976 Ford, but I also am not too fussed on a lot of plot. I’d love to find more movies that take place in a small enclosed world of their own - think submarine, cruise ship, space station, ruined Lithuanian mansion, whatever. I like a small-ish set of characters and something visually interesting (though I’m happy for that to be landscapes or a 60s style spaceship, don’t need high tech, high res, cgi or anything) that plays out in that one closed ecosystem. Loved the original Death on The Nile, enjoyed Hunt for Red October, big fan of Wes Anderson, period dramas, etc. Happy to do subtitles, not a fan of extreme body horror/torture/action movies, but anything else is fine. Would love a good movie on a space ship or something more cerebral and literary. I realize this is a bit all over the place, figured I’d give it a try and see if there a brilliant Poirot meets Jane Eyre meets Wes Anderson movie filmed entirely in a decrepit train station I somehow missed.

### Top 10 comments and their replies (up to depth 10)

1. [removed]
2. Local Hero (1983)
3. Moon (2009), Snowpiercer (2013), Biosphere (2022), The Outfit (2022), Life of Pi (2012)
4. Banshees of Inisherin (2022)
5. Kontroll
6. Passengers 2016

Space Station 76

Moon

Enter Nowhere

Clue

The Breakfast Club
7. Cube (1997) 
8. The Black Hole
9. Train Dreams
10. Portrait of a Lady on Fire

Sentimental Value

The Zone of Interest

The Banshees of Inisherin (assuming you count an island as an enclosed world)

Women Talking

Also, just in terms of vibe, even if it doesn't quite fit "enclosed world" as much, Drive My Car seems like it would fit what you're after!

---

# Top 5 posts from r/moviecritic day

## 1. Proof that age doesn't define great filmmaking.

**Score:** 1870

### Top 6 comments and their replies (up to depth 10)

1. Spielberg was 27 when he directed Jaws, Tarintino was 28 when he did Reservoir Dogs.


Youth has always been a good place for innovation, even if the industry doesn't give non established directors a chance.
  - PTA writing/directing Boogie Nights at 26 has always been kinda mind blowing to me too
    - Orson Welles was 25 when he wrote, directed and starred in Citizen Kane.
  - Hollywood unfortunately is not a very easy place to work in. Speaking from first hand experience. A lot of the times it operates on the five Fs:

Friends, family, finances, favors, and fucking. 
2. Also proof of:


1. Survivorship bias


2. Horror audiences rewarding ideas over name and status
3. Christopher Nolan was only in his 30s when he made these masterpieces

https://preview.redd.it/61nll3k35f5h1.jpeg?width=1440&format=pjpg&auto=webp&s=26c124cd631126e13d9fb6c327614bb071e3a9a2
  - You left out Memento... Nolan was only 29 when it was produced.

4. No one ever said it did 
  - I think the young crowd doesn't realize that these are not unprecedented times, but they dont know about recent film history so they THINK this is more special than it is

not to knock the movies or filmmakers themselves
5. This isn’t the issue. The issue is young filmmakers not being hired more in Hollywood. Executives who are the viral plague of the industry always hire old timers and never give newer filmmakers a chance. 

Netflix broke this tradition but sadly has reverted to the studios style since last year 

## 2. What’s the best movie of the 21st century so far?

**Score:** 967

### Top 8 comments and their replies (up to depth 10)

1. ![gif](giphy|wD7BsjK5zqLew)
  - ![gif](giphy|1BZSEGf9nGlScdksrc)
2. *In the Mood for Love*

![gif](giphy|3o6YgdSwAEr6cJTfWw)
3. ![gif](giphy|ukE9oP3kb7TVu)
4. City of God
5. ![gif](giphy|MXoz4MqhUYinsJ5bEz|downsized)
  - Love all three! My favorite is Hot Fuzz, but cannot go wrong with any of them. 
6. The LOTR trilogy. Obviously 
  - And I would add that they still only count as one. 
7. ![gif](giphy|rNgT8P8pL3dn2)

## 3. Which actor did you initially not rate, but in one performance they proved you totally wrong?

**Score:** 674
**Body:** For me it was Kristen Stewart. Her performance in Spencer was so impressive, in my opinion. Nuanced, fragile, empathetic, and captured a real  
person’s mannerisms. She even nailed the accent. It completely altered my view of her as an actress.

Who’s yours?

### Top 7 comments and their replies (up to depth 10)

1. I already liked him, but Colin Farrell as penguin took it up a notch. (Almost makes me forgive him for Alexander)
  - Watch In Bruges
2. I always thought Jason Isaacs was pretty one-note, but he blew me away playing against type in Death of Stalin. I look forward to seeing him pop up in anything, and I’m glad he’s getting some more diverse roles now beyond “snide, villainous aristocrat” (though he does admittedly crush it when playing that archetype).
3. ![gif](giphy|F0A48Q2wFjE7S)

I honestly thought this was the worst casting idea of all time. I stand corrected
  - We all did. Then were proven wrong. Then Jared Leto got the part and we were like “ok fuck this but maybe it’ll be good because Heath Ledger” and it was not good at all.
4. Also Twilight-related, Robert Pattinson. His performances in The Lighthouse and (to a lesser extent) The Batman are mind-blowing.
  - He’s great in The Devil All the Time 
5. Not that he’s ever been bad but Adam Sandler in uncut gems was so different from his usual characters
  - He reminds us he's a great actor once a decade and then he goes back to the basketball shorts and makes another swimming pool of money. 
6. Ryan Gosling

![gif](giphy|l0MYDSv9mzn6Rar8A)

## 4. Goodbye, Streaming. Physical Media forever

**Score:** 629
**Body:** DVDs >>> Netflix 

### Top 6 comments and their replies (up to depth 10)

1. Me and my wife have probably 600-800 in our collection so far. Slowly switching over everything from Blu-Ray to 4k depending on how well they did it. Movies like Terminator 2, the 4k remaster doesn’t look so great.
  - ![gif](giphy|t6cn3lRhDZtBjdAjKN)
    - That’s gotta be a Mandela effect for people. That’s actually his sister. Never understood why they used the “my wiiiife” when he’s standing next to his sister. 
      - ![gif](giphy|DZyxZgmcbC264)
2. The Simpsons, a man of culture 
3. Perks of physical media: deleted scenes, alternate endings, director's cut, and cast/director commentary. I prefer physical media due to these perks.
4. Got my first Blu ray player in December, been collecting all sorts since. I love the Collector's Editions. I just prefer physical media and have been delighted to return to it, but I still use streaming to rent movies before buying them if I love them.
5. Personally, I’m more Pirating > streaming platforms, but physical media is great too. I’m sick of paying money to my digital land barren.
  - I use a hearty mix of both. I go the cinema a lot and buy things I really love, but pirate unashamedly since streaming services are too expensive, too low quality, and can't be relied on to provide secure access to what you want to watch on them
  - I love being a data hoarding pira....er... conscientious digital media archivist. 

## 5. What are some movies that are 3 hours or longer that kept you engaged throughout the movie?

**Score:** 507
**Body:** These are just some that come to mind for me.

### Top 9 comments and their replies (up to depth 10)

1. https://preview.redd.it/4dho2rfjae5h1.jpeg?width=1289&format=pjpg&auto=webp&s=3f74b582a1d021f8dc7cc0d2ca7f10be2f31b87f

The Lord of The Rings trilogy

Even the Extended Editions (at least Fellowship and Two Towers) kept me engaged throughout the majority of the movies.  On the flip side, I think all three Hobbit movies are shorter than the LotR movies but they all feel longer.
  - The Hobbit movies are like butter spread over too much bread.
2. Das boot
3. The first one that comes to mind is Return of the King
4. [The Stand](https://boxd.it/1PZU) (1994)

It was an eight hour long, four part miniseries.  I absolutely loved it, as a kid.  I still watch it every couple of years.
5. Bladerunner 2049
  - I didn't even realise it was that long.
6. ![gif](giphy|vE9elHKIYgGzu)
7. Watchmen
8. ![gif](giphy|fTbOTnSNzXd2o)

Boogie Nights

# Top 5 posts from r/TrueFilm day

## 1. The Decline of Nudity in US Film: Why Did Hollywood Stop Treating Sex as a Normal Part of Adult Life? [Essay]

**Score:** 96
**Body:** **Note***: This began as a reply to another thread asking why there seems to be less nudity in contemporary American film than there was from the 1970s through the 1990s. The response ended up growing far longer than I intended, and it seemed worth posting separately. I’m curious to hear your reactions and counterpoints. Thanks!:*

*———————————————————-*

It’s become common to the point of banality to observe that, for the better part of the past quarter century, nudity has been slowly disappearing from mainstream U.S. films. The most common explanations tend to focus on concrete, pragmatic concerns about actor contracts, international markets, intimacy coordinators, and the practical realities of filmmaking. All of those factors matter, to be sure, but I find myself increasingly interested in this subject less as an isolated market-driven phenomenon than as a kind of microcosmic reflection of larger changes within U.S. culture: shifting attitudes about sexuality and danger, the collapse of adult-oriented drama, and a changing relationship between public and private life. 

If you go to international film festivals—personally I try to hit Palm Springs International Film Festival every year as they show work from all over the globe that doesn’t necessarily get play at the larger festivals—you will notice more common artistic use of nudity than in U.S. films. Belgian, French, Japanese, Korean, Spanish, Nordic, and even Indian and Iranian films all tend to have more nudity. I cannot readily bring to mind a German dramatic film I have seen in the past decade that doesn’t use nudity in some substantial way, though there are also specific cultural reasons for that. German culture’s emphasis on social liberation around nudity is partly a conscious effort at permanently abandoning the sort of cultural and artistic conservatism of both the late 19th century and the Third Reich. They seem watchful against prudery in much the same way they are watchful about hyperinflation.

Too, and for all that it can be overstated, America does retain a puritanical impulse that goes back to before the nation’s founding. It is often a shape-shifting animal though. Along with the positive reforms brought about by #MeToo came a broader cultural tendency to treat nudity itself as potentially suspect or problematic. The distinction between exploitation and representation sometimes seems to have become blurred. Concerns about consent and coercion on set are obviously legitimate, but they can coexist with a more general discomfort around the body that is not necessarily progressive.

I think the 1970s is key here, because that’s the era when American film broke with the Hollywood studio system and became unusually auteur-driven. There was a much stronger conception of filmmakers and actors as artists first and celebrities second. In some circles, appearing nude in a serious film might have enhanced one’s artistic credibility, while appearing in a commercial advertisement could damage it. Today, that hierarchy has been almost completely reversed. For many performers, there seems to have been a growing skepticism of the idea that filmmaking is somehow more noble than any other capitalist enterprise. There’s a fascinating irony in the fact that, while socialism has seen a resurgence (at least rhetorically) in U.S. culture, we are also more comfortable with commercialization and consumerism than ever before. The air quotes and sense of revulsion that once accompanied terms like “personal brand” have largely disappeared in an age when social media has forced all artists to act as the commercial promoters of their own work. 

The 1970s is, in my opinion, the most interesting era of American film because it broadly rejects the kinds of narrative assumptions that often feel innate to American culture. There is a freedom that comes from a willingness to discard convention. I recently rewatched *Deliverance*, a staple of that period, and was struck by how thoroughly it resists the impulse to make a hero—or even an antihero—out of any of its characters. That particular film does not contain much nudity—though it does have one famously graphic if unimpeachably artistically justified scene of sexual assault—but I think the attitude is nonetheless telling. The same sensibility that allows a filmmaker to dispense with conventional heroes also tends to produce a more relaxed attitude toward depicting sexuality, the body, and other aspects of adult life.

I also wonder whether the AIDS crisis deserves more attention in this discussion than it typically receives. The sexual revolution of the 1960s and 70s produced a culture that often understood sex as liberatory, joyful, and even politically meaningful. Even some pornographic films became the subject of legitimate mainstream artistic and cultural discussion in a way that’s hard to imagine today. In particular, *Deep Throat*, *Behind the Green Door*, and *The Devil in Miss Jones* all entered the cultural mainstream lexicon, becoming legitimate subjects for discussion in artistic and academic circles. Today, we have, in my view, regressed to a borderline-Victorian relationship with pornography and sexually explicit content: it’s happening everywhere and virtually everyone consumes it, but people almost universally feign ignorance of its particulars. Tangentially, I suspect this has negatively impacted the artistic ambition of the adult films being made.

The AIDS crisis did not simply alter behavior; it changed the emotional and symbolic meaning of sex in American life. By the late 1980s and 1990s, sex increasingly carried associations with danger, vulnerability, disease, and mortality. That cultural shift was certainly not universal, but I suspect it had consequences that extended well beyond public health. It is difficult to imagine that a society’s artistic output would remain entirely untouched by such a profound change in how intimacy was perceived.

I would also return to the point about the 1970s being an unusually auteur-driven period. When people think of nudity in that era, they often think of transgression, but what strikes me is how frequently it simply appears as part of adult drama in a matter-of-fact way. Films like *McCabe & Mrs. Miller*, *Klute*, *Shampoo*, *The Last Picture Show*, and *Don’t Look Now* do not generally treat sexuality as a special event requiring narrative justification. It is simply part of the texture of adulthood. **Kubrick** and **Altman** are particularly interesting in this regard because they often seem unconcerned with whether a scene will make an audience comfortable. Their films frequently give the impression that the director is pursuing an artistic question rather than attempting to satisfy an audience expectation or social norm. If anything, there may be a slight bias in their work towards rejecting social expectations, since film in the period is, in some respects, fundamentally understood as a product of the counterculture.

Lastly, I think the larger decline of the adult-oriented dramatic film is probably a major factor. Many of the movies that once contained this kind of nudity were mid-budget dramas made for grown-ups. That category of film has largely collapsed in the American theatrical marketplace. The kinds of stories that might once have become a feature film are now often made as prestige television. One could argue that American culture hasn’t become uniformly more prudish. Rather, many of the venues where adult sexuality was once depicted have migrated elsewhere to other forms of media. If one looks at contemporary television rather than studio films, much of that material is still there. The fact that it has moved out of the public sphere of the cinema and into the private home is also rife with potential lines of inquiry, but I’ve gone on long enough.

**One final thought:**

This didn’t necessarily fit easily anywhere else in the piece, but I felt like it was worth appending here. Personally speaking, I think the use of digital technology and body doubles has somewhat undermined the artistic legitimacy of nudity in mainstream film and prestige television. For example, Lena Headey’s famous “Walk of Shame” sequence on *Game of Thrones* was actually performed by **Rebecca Van Cleave**, her body double, and then digitally altered to attach Headey’s face to Van Cleave’s body. I think it is perfectly reasonable for an actor to choose not to perform nude scenes, but there is something rather cowardly about having another performer assume the physical and emotional burden of the scene while receiving little of the artistic recognition for it. That is partly why I wanted to name **Van Cleave** here rather than simply referring to her as “Headey’s body double.”

### Top 8 comments and their replies (up to depth 10)

1. This is a good start, but it’s missing one of the major material reasons why nudity started declining in American films. 

As the cost of film production rose throughout the 70s and 80s, and studios began chasing blockbuster profits, films needed to appeal to as large an audience as possible. Thus, starting in the 80s, films were made to appeal to as many of the “four quadrants” as possible. This in turn raised production costs further, as the easiest guarantee to hit those four quadrants were family-friendly adventure films, which often cost a great deal in location shooting, star power, and special effects. It also meant that overt sexuality and nudity were a no go, if studios wanted families in middle America bringing their pre-teen children.

In the search for more viewers, studios looked abroad to sell their films to massive audiences in Asia and South America. The problem was that many of these countries had fairly restrictive censorship regimes, which meant that the films Americans could sell to distributors in those countries had to be fairly anodyne. Thus - an even greater reduction in nudity and sexuality, especially in expensive studio productions.
  - I appreciate you raising these points! I think the original discussion that inspired the post focused heavily on market dynamics, so I somewhat neglected that in my own analysis, but there’s probably a broader way to fit that into the cultural discussion. 
  - 80s feels like too early for when a lack of sex happened. Even if it was sanitized, most 80s movies had sex as a central plot point. The producers even added in the sex scene in Top Gun after seeing the rough cut. And the 90s were filled with erotic movies. 

Sex being in movies declined throughout the 00s.
2. If you're interested in this topic I'd recommend the podcast *You Must Remember This* by Karina Longworth, it covers a variety of topics related to the history of film in the 20th century, but one season called "Erotic '80s" and its sequel, "Erotic '90s", cover the evolution of sexuality and gender in Hollywood from the implementation of the X rating to the release of *Eyes Wide Shut* in 1999, hitting on everything from teen sexploitation and the AIDS crisis to the tribulations of the NC-17 rating and the Lolita craze of the Nineties.
  - These are my favorite two series that she’s done for that podcast and that’s saying something because literally everything Karina Longworth does is great. The ones about the Hollywood Blacklist and the Charles Manson era are also top tier. 
3. Two thoughts to add to this analysis, both informed by my personal experience (they're not anecdotes, they're small-batch artisanal data!):

First, I directed an indie feature in 2022. It had some nudity in it. Of course we hired an intimacy coordinator, who did an amazing job, and my actors felt completely comfortable and safe the whole time. It was a great experience and it made the movie better as the connection between the actors felt extremely real and intense. 10/10, would hire an IC again.

But having been through that experience as a director, I can no longer fathom how movies did it before IC's came along. Literally every appearance of nudity in film before IC's feels suspicious to me now, even kissing! It all feels like a bunch of dudes using their power to shoot naked pretty girls doing sexy things. And yes, I know every actress who did them will tell you they were fine with it, it wasn't coerced, and I believe them, but there is something so structurally unbalanced about the whole setup without an IC, even if everyone is agreeing to it on set. It feels ripe for abuse, even unintentionally. It's like that Alec Baldwin incident with the gun on set - even if he didn't mean to shoot those people, the conditions of the set led to that outcome. You cut the corner so you don't have to pay the IC or you're rushing through things and while you might save money, it's very easy for an actor to come out of that feeling icky, even if in the moment they said they were cool with it.

One of the things you learn in film school is not to write something unless you have a plan to film it safely, and at film school the three things we were told to be wary of were stunts, children, and animals - the paperwork on all of those was so laborious and the coordinators you need so expensive that many people would just retool their projects to avoid those pitfalls. I think intimacy has become a 4th part of that soup - at the point it costs you an extra 500 bucks to have someone on the set for the day, you'd be amazed at just how creative you can get trying to avoid that.

And for what? It used to be that if you had no money and no stars, having a pretty girl get naked in your indie movie was a great and cheap way to get some attention for your project. "There will be nudity" was a selling point for a lot of those sexy adult dramas! Now it's a whole process and it doesn't move the needle.

Secondly, Gen Z generally HATES nudity/intimacy in movies. I taught filmmaking to teens for years and by and large those kiddos are SUPER uninterested. They also watch movies differently - mostly at home, with their families nearby, on iPads or phones. They don't really go to the movie theater, and especially don't go alone. They don't wander freely like we used to, they don't have video stores to explore and look at tape covers, etc. So pretty much anywhere they watch a movie they are doing it with their mom nearby or something. And the streaming service doesn't want just one person watching, they want mom watching WITH them, so it's a lot of family-appropriate content like Steven Universe that can also be marketed in other ways (those adult dramas don't have a lot of collectible figurines).

On top of that, they grew up in MeToo to the point where they are very good at seeing through shady people, commercial porn is easily accessible, they take intimate selfies and risk revenge porn from exes, they already have a crapton of anxiety about most things in life, and they are FAR more queer/genderfluid than previous generations. All this to say, issues of intimacy, nudity, and sex are complicated, thorny issues for them, with lots of subtext, risk, vulnerability, and fear, and they're more interested in critiquing and deconstructing those ideas than they are in having a great time looking at beautiful sexy bodies.

As part of that critique they are INCREDIBLY dismissive of nudity (many times wrongly, IMO) as being gratuitous, prurient, etc. and don't want their movies to be horny at all. They want their movies to be easygoing and fun, and while watching naked babes was fun for decades, it isn't so much for the young people today.

And this is going to continue for a while. Gen Z is now entering that vaunted 18-34 demographic (I know, I know, it sucks "being from the 1900's" as they say), so the people who make movies need to cater to their wishes for a while in order to stay afloat.
4. I always assumed there was more nudity in films of that time because they almost had to have an overcorrection of the previous 4 decades of films being censored by the Hayes code. 

Before the 70’s, films weren’t really allowed to show whatever they wanted, so filmmakers took advantage of it when they finally got the chance to do whatever they wanted. 

Of course, over time, it became more of the norm and it’s like filmmakers didn’t feel the need to almost rebel in a sense based on how censored films once were. 
5. Blockbusters lack nudity because they have to be PG13 for the widest market. R rated movies still have nudity frequently, just less of the tacky T&A that was a low effort draw before everyone could just watch porn on their phones.
6. This is one of the more thoughtful analyses I've read on this topic, and I think it justifies being its own post, but it still suffers what I think is always the biggest gap in these discussions, which is to say when we talk about sex and nudity in film we're mostly talking about *female* nudity for the assumed benefit of a *heterosexual male* audience. It seems reductive to me to dismiss all distaste for on-screen nudity as prudishness when I think a large part of that distaste can be traced directly or indirectly to that disparity rather than a dislike of sex or nudity in general. Of course there's nothing inherently misogynist about nudity in film, but American cinema has very often used nudity in misogynist ways, or at least ways that emphasized men's dominance in the industry, and that creates a lasting association.

That said, I think you end on potentially the most intriguing question. What does it mean for nudity to fall out of favour in art cinema, but exist in unprecedented levels on television. I also think there's been a shift of sexuality from the public sphere to the private sphere (largely driven by online pornography) but then how to account for the fact that extremely explicit TV shows like Game of Thrones or Euphoria that could never have been aired 30 years ago are water cooler TV for the generations that are supposedly scared of sex scenes?
7. So I was thinking about this while watching DRESSED TO KILL (1980) for the first time early last month. From the dawn of movies until the widespread acceptance of the internet in the early 00’s, it was much harder to see moving images of nudity or sex. I believe nudity began to disappear from film as it became easier to access in other areas. 

My midpoint for the theory is SWORDFISH. Halle Berry being topless was a selling point for the film. This was an attempt to compete with the advance of online adult content (look, she’s FAMOUS!) and was a ploy to distract from a bad movie (much like dressed to kill having a nude Angie Dickinson 5 seconds into the film) 

I’m sure I’m wrong, and people in this thread are much smarter and well-versed in film theory than I am, but I was my initial reaction to this question a few weeks back, and I wanted to share. 

## 2. Videodrome: The Most Genius Use of Body Horror

**Score:** 34
**Body:** It’s been years since I’ve watched this film (and honestly, I think I’ll need to watch again) and despite being a brilliant exploration of the impossibility of distinguishing between reality and the representation in a world fuelled by media outlets that hijack our cognitive framework, the use of body horror is really quite brilliant. 

By making animate objects (humans) interconnected with inanimate objects (technology), the animate and inanimate become inseparable, ultimately making it impossible to distinguish between human and technology. It’s such a brilliant way of using body-horror as a metaphor for technology becoming inseparable that has obviously become more and more potent since the films release. Brilliant movie!

I really love the way he uses body horror to explore the way media and tech influence our dissociation from reality in eXistenZ, the exploration of the role of the body and medical anxiety in grief and the fear of death in Shrouds, and the relation between artist, his work, and its viewers/critics as well as hedonism of the ultra-rich and how it contributes to modern vanity culture in Crimes of the Future.

One of the greatest contemporary directors.

## 4. Girl With The Needle

**Score:** 5
**Body:** Loved the lush, elegant black and white. A mysterious, tension filled story, and solid performances that don’t call attention to themselves. You could credibly call it horror but that’s not what I walked away with. 

I’m ambivalent about the ending,  but I haven’t finished processing it so I’ll withhold judgement. 

Based loosely on a true story, The adverb in that description would lead me to think that the story has been altered in a way that makes it neater and probably leaves out the interesting bits. The thought that this film toned down the real events only makes it more horrifying.

There are some exquisitely brutal moments here, all carried out off screen. The knowledge, the very idea of  what’s happening was more than enough to make me squirm. 

An allegory is clearly intended that could have been developed more, and the ending is questionable. Still,it’s strong enough that I didn’t dare glance at my phone or grab a snack. It’s one beautiful scene after another, and the unnamed source of dread is haunting. 

I can understand how this film might be polarizing. I’m firmly on team “loved it”
